text: The surgeon performs a wedge resection of the diverticulum on the anti-mesenteric surface of the colon using cautery. The polyp is visible beneath the surgeon's left hand instrument as they excise the tissue around the diverticulum. The surgeon carefully navigates around other diverticula, cutting across them as needed for the resection.

The specimen is separated using three 5mm ports. The gastroenterologist removes the specimen through the colon via a natural orifice. The surgeon examines the defect, revealing a distal diverticulum that has been partially excised.

The surgeon divides a bridge of tissue to open the lumen of the colon, stabilizing the bowel with a 3-0 silk suture on the far lateral aspect. The defect is visible as a small opening on the left anterior aspect of the sigmoid colon. A V-lock suture is used to close the defect.

The surgeon inserts a needle through a five-millimeter trocar, causing a slight bend at the tip, but proceeds with suturing the bowel wall using an intracorporeal technique. The inner layer is sutured in an over-and-over fashion, followed by imbricating the tissue down, similar to closing the common channel in an intracorporeal anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the closure of the bowel wall in two layers using silk sutures, tying the final stitch to the stay suture positioned laterally. The surgical field is examined for bleeding and luminal narrowing, followed by a scope insertion to confirm the patency of the lumen. The proximal colon appears distended due to gas insufflation, but no leaks are observed.
